relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Six days before Passover, Jesus comes to Bethany where Lazarus lives after being raised.
Martha serves, and Lazarus reclines at table.
Mary fills the house with costly fragrance at Jesus’ feet.
Judas, the coming betrayer, objects.
He masks his concern in language about the poor.
His words hide theft rather than care.
Jesus protects Mary’s act as burial-sign.
The poor remain present, but this moment is singular.
The crowd comes to see both Jesus and Lazarus.
The plot extends even toward Lazarus.
Lazarus’ living witness draws many into trust.
The feast crowd hears Jesus is coming to Jerusalem.
Palms and Hosanna greet the coming King of Israel.
Jesus sits on the young donkey.
Daughter Zion is told not to fear her humble king.
Only after glory do the disciples remember the Scripture-pattern.
Those who saw Lazarus called out of the tomb bear witness.
The sign draws the crowd to meet him.
The Pharisees say the world has gone after him.
Greeks come up to worship at the feast.
They ask Philip: we wish to see Jesus.
Philip and Andrew carry the desire to Jesus.
The hour has come for the Son of Humanity to be glorified.
The grain must fall and die to bear much fruit.
Life clutched is lost; life surrendered is guarded into the age.
Serving means following into where he is.
His soul is troubled, yet the hour is why he came.
The Father’s name is glorified and will be glorified again.
The crowd hears thunder or messenger.
The voice is for them.
The world-order’s judgment and ruler-casting begin now.
Lifted up, he draws all to himself.
Lifted-up language points to the death to come.
The crowd struggles to reconcile lifted Son of Humanity with enduring Messiah.
Walk while the light remains among you.
Trust into the light and become children of light.
Many signs are seen, yet trust is withheld.
Isaiah’s report and revealed arm become the interpretive frame.
The refusal of trust is read through Isaiah again.
Eyes, heart, turning, and healing are held in prophetic tension.
Isaiah saw glory and spoke concerning him.
Some rulers trust secretly under synagogue fear.
Human glory is loved more than Divine glory.
Trust in Jesus reaches the Sender.
Seeing Jesus beholds the Sender.
Light comes so trust need not remain in darkness.
He comes not to judge the world but to save/heal it.
The spoken word itself judges rejection in the last day.
He speaks from the Father’s command, not from himself.
The Father’s command is age-long life.
